P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
(916) 322·5662 
Walter V. Hays 
Town Attorney 
Town of Los Gatos 
P.O. Box 949 
Administration 
322·5660 
Los Gatos, CA 
95030 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
Enforcement 
322·6"''''1 
August 16, 1982 
Re: 
Brent ventura; Follow-up on Advice 
Letter No. A-82-083 
Our No. A-82-143 
Dear Mr. Hays: 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Thank you for your recent letter following up on a series 
of advice letters regarding City Councilman Brent Ventura. 
Reference is made to our letters, A-82-083, A-82-063 and 
A-80-090, each of which is incorporated herein by reference. 
By way of recapitulation, Mr. Ventura owns an interest in 
real property in excess of $1,000. 
It is in the form of part 
ownership of a seven unit apartment complex in the Town of 
Los Gatos. 
The rent control ordinance extension decision 
which is pending will foreseeably have some effect upon the 
rental income derived from Mr. Ventura's real property 
interest. 
The question is one of whether the foreseeable 
effect will be material. 
In the previous letters, 
materiality was present as to some decisions and not as to 
some others. 
Mr. Ventura now requests our advice with respect to 
changes in the facts that our previous letters were based 
upon. 
The monthly rents from the seven apartments previously 
totaled $1,400-1,600/month. 
Based upon that fact, a material 
financial effect would be $70-80/month, total, or roughly $10 
to $13 per unit per month. 
Now, Mr. Ventura reports that the 
rent has risen to $1,788 total per month. 
A 5% increase 
would then be $90 per month, or roughly $15 per unit per 
month. 
Therefore, if the difference of having or not having 
the rent control ordinance will change Mr. Ventura's rents on 


Walter V. Hays 
August 16, 1982 
Page 2 
his apartments by less than $15 per unit per month, he would 
have no conflict of interest. 
If it is reasonably fore-
seeable that the difference will exceed $15 per unit per 
month, then he must disqualify himself. 
If we assume a 
concomitant increase in the value of the property (from 
$400,000 to $450,000), then the effect on its value by virtue 
of the ordinance continuing or expiring would have to be less 
than $2,250; otherwise the effect of the decision would be 
material and disqualification would be required. 
I trust that this letter satisfactorily responds to 
Mr. Ventura's question regarding the changed circumstances. 
As always, it is left up to you and he, who are familiar with 
the facts locally, to determine what the reasonably fore-
seeable effect of the decision will be on Mr. Ventura's 
interest in the apartment complex. 
If you have any 
questions, I may be reached at (916) 322-5901.

REL:plh 


July 21, 1982 
Robert E. Leidigh, Esq. 
Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804 
Re: 
Brent Ventura; Follow-Up of Advice Letter 82-083 
Dear Mr. Leidigh: 
In September, 1982, the Town Catmcil will be considering a temporary, three-month 
extension of the ordinance identified in our earlier correspondence, and Mr. 
VentUt"a has again asked if he TIllst disqualify himself. 
On July 20, 1982, in 
response to your letter of May l3, 1982, Mr. Ventura advised Ire that the gross 
rents on the apartrrent are now $1,788/month as compared to $1,400-$1,600/mmth 
at the time of your July 17, 1980 letter. 
I have given Mr. Ventura my own opinion on the subject, a copy of which is en-
closed. 
Because he is under strong pressUt"e to vote, however, he has also re-
quested that I obtain a brief formal opinion from you. 
Thank you again for your assistance on this matter. 
VNH:ms 
cc: 
Brent VentUt"a 
Sincerely yours, 
Walter V. Hays 
ToWl Attorney 


July 21, 1982 
To: 
Brent Ventura 
Fran: Walt Hays 
Re: 
Vote m Rent Mediatim Ordinance 
Issue 
Given the fact that the Fair Political Practices Cornmissim (FPPC) has already 
ruled that your interest in a family trust disqualified you fran voting for 
adoption of the rent rnediatim ordinance when it was first proposed, would you 
also be disqualified fram voting for (1) permanent adoptim, or (2) a brief 
extensim? 
Sunmary Answer 
You are disqualified to vote m either permanent adoptim or a brief extension 
of the ordinance, because a vote not to extend the ordinance would cause it 
to lapse, and it is reasmably foreseeable that such a decisim would have a 
IlE.terial financial effect, distinguishable fram its effect m the public 
generally, or your property interests. 
In his letter to me of "May 13, 1982, :MY. Leidigh of the FPPC suggested that if 
the value of your property interest had increased sufficiently, it might be 
possible that your vote m extensim of the ordinance would not have a material 
effect m it. In my opinim, however, the revised rent figures you submitted to 
me on July 20, 1982, do not indicate a sufficient increase to enable Mr. Leidigh's 
exception to apply. 
Per your request, I have asked Mr. Leidigh to give us his fOrIlE.l opinim m the 
issue.

sections.conclusion: Therefore, if the difference of having or not having the rent control ordinance will change Mr. Ventura's rents on his apartments by less than $15 per unit per month, he would have no conflict of interest. If it is reasonably foreseeable that the difference will exceed $15 per unit per month, then he must disqualify himself. If we assume a concomitant increase in the value of the property (from $400,000 to $450,000), then the effect on its value by virtue of the ordinance continuing or expiring would have to be less than $2,250; otherwise the effect of the decision would be material and disqualification would be required.
sections.question_synthetic: Given that Councilman Brent Ventura's rental income from an apartment complex has increased from $1,400-1,600/month to $1,788/month, does he have a material financial conflict of interest requiring disqualification from voting on a rent control ordinance extension?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Ventura has a conflict of interest requiring disqualification if the ordinance's effect on his rents exceeds $15 per unit per month or on property value exceeds $2,250, based on updated financial figures. The FPPC establishes quantitative thresholds for materiality based on the changed financial circumstances.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains FPPC advice letter (A-82-143) dated August 16, 1982, responding to a request from Town Attorney Walter Hays regarding updated facts. Question and conclusion sections clearly identified. Also includes supporting correspondence from July 21, 1982 (incoming request from Hays and town attorney opinion to Ventura). Main FPPC response is well-structured with quantitative guidance.

If it is reasonably foreseeable that the difference will exceed $15 per unit per month, then he must disqualify himself. embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel responds to a follow-up inquiry regarding Councilman Brent Ventura's potential conflict of interest in voting on a rent control ordinance extension, establishing specific materiality thresholds based on updated rental income figures and property valuation.
